description: Figure 9 presents a strength-elongation 'banana diagram' comparing the mechanical property ranges of various low-density steel categories, with ultimate tensile strength (MPa) on the x-axis and total elongation (%) on the y-axis. The plot displays five distinct material groups: Fe-Al ferritic steels (red ellipse, ~500-650 MPa, ~15-30% elongation), Fe-Mn-Al-C duplex steels (α+γ, blue ellipse, ~600-800 MPa, ~35-45% elongation), Fe-Mn-Al-C precipitation-hardening (PH) steels (α+κ, blue ellipse, ~800-900 MPa, ~10-20% elongation), Fe-Mn-Al-C triplex steels (γ+α+κ, blue ellipse, ~850-1000 MPa, ~40-70% elongation), and Fe-Al intermetallics (purple ellipse, ~950-1000 MPa, ~5-15% elongation). Density reduction percentages are indicated as 7.5-12.7% for ferritic steels, 10-20% for Fe-Mn-Al-C steels, and >25% for intermetallics. The figure demonstrates that austenite-containing duplex and triplex steels achieve superior strength-ductility balances compared to ferritic steels while offering significant density reductions, supporting their potential for automotive lightweighting applications.
